title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about traditional crafts and artisans. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: Made by Hand
content.subtitle: An article about the revival of traditional crafts.
content.text:
In a converted railway arch in south London, a woman named Delia is bent over a workbench, repairing a nineteenth-century chair with techniques that have not fundamentally changed since the chair was first made. She learned her craft from her father, who learned from his. The tools on her bench — the webbing stretcher, the curved needle, the mallets of various sizes — are the same tools that would have furnished an upholsterer's workshop two centuries ago. [31] To watch Delia work is to observe a form of knowledge transmission that exists entirely outside the digital age, passed on not through screens or manuals but through hands and repetition. [/31]

For much of the twentieth century, trades like hers were in steep decline. Industrial manufacturing made handmade goods expensive and slow by comparison, and the cultural prestige of the craftsperson gave way to the engineer and the programmer. [32] Craft schools closed, apprenticeships dwindled, and entire bodies of specialist knowledge — how to blow glass, how to throw a pot to precise tolerances, how to beat copper into a vessel — came within a generation of disappearing entirely. [/32]

The revival that has taken hold over the past two decades is therefore all the more striking. Craft schools are reopening. Waiting lists for pottery, silversmithing, and weaving courses have grown to years in some cases. [33] Analysts who study consumer behaviour attribute this in part to what they call the 'reaction to the virtual': as more of daily life migrates online, the appeal of objects made with physical skill and imperfect by design has intensified considerably. [/33] A handmade bowl bears the marks of its maker's decisions. It cannot be replicated exactly. In a world of infinite reproductions, this becomes a form of value in itself.

For the artisans themselves, the return of public interest has been both welcome and complicated. Many are careful to distinguish between a genuine craft revival and what they call 'craft aesthetics' — the marketing of machine-made goods in the vocabulary of handmade authenticity. [34] 'There are companies selling "artisan" bread that is produced in factories at enormous scale,' says Marcus, a stonemason in York. 'The word has been colonised. People think they're buying into a tradition when they're often buying into branding.' [/34]

The deeper question is whether traditional crafts can survive commercially without compromising the very qualities that make them valuable. Some artisans charge prices that reflect the true cost of hand work — and find customers who understand and accept that. Others have adapted, using modern tools for preparatory stages while reserving handcraft for the elements that define the finished piece. [35] Neither approach is considered pure by everyone in the craft community, but both have allowed skills to survive that might otherwise have vanished. [/35]

Delia herself is pragmatic. She does not romanticise her work or pretend it is the only way to restore furniture. What she offers, she says, is a conversation with the original maker — an attempt to understand the decisions behind every joint and tuck, and to honour them in the repair. [36] 'When I finish a piece,' she says quietly, 'you shouldn't be able to tell where the old work ends and mine begins. That's not modesty — that's the point of the craft.' [/36]

questions.question: The description of Delia's workshop is used to illustrate that
options: A) traditional crafts are outdated and inefficient.
B) craft knowledge is preserved and transmitted through direct practical experience.
C) modern artisans use the same tools as their ancestors without improvement.
D) upholstery is the most historically significant of all crafts.
questions.explanation: The passage describes craft knowledge as passed on 'not through screens or manuals but through hands and repetition', emphasising practical, direct transmission.

questions.question: The writer's main point in the second paragraph is that
options: A) industrial manufacturing produced better-quality goods than craftspeople.
B) traditional craft knowledge came very close to being lost entirely.
C) craft schools failed because they could not attract enough students.
D) programmers and engineers replaced craftspeople in cultural importance.
questions.explanation: The paragraph describes how entire bodies of specialist knowledge 'came within a generation of disappearing entirely', showing how close traditional crafts were to being lost.

questions.question: According to consumer behaviour analysts, the recent revival of interest in crafts is partly because
options: A) handmade goods are now cheaper than mass-produced alternatives.
B) people are attracted to imperfect objects in reaction to how much of their lives is now digital.
C) craft schools have successfully marketed their courses to young people.
D) manufacturers have begun to replicate the appearance of handmade goods.
questions.explanation: Analysts describe a 'reaction to the virtual': as life moves online, 'the appeal of objects made with physical skill and imperfect by design has intensified'.

questions.question: Marcus the stonemason is quoted in order to show that
options: A) bakers have benefited most from the craft revival.
B) the word 'artisan' has lost its meaning through commercial misuse.
C) consumers do not understand the difference between craft and factory work.
D) artisans should be legally protected from companies using misleading labels.
questions.explanation: Marcus says 'The word has been colonised' by companies selling factory goods as 'artisan', showing the term has been emptied of its original meaning.

questions.question: The writer's attitude towards artisans who use some modern tools is
options: A) critical, as it undermines the authenticity of the craft.
B) indifferent, as such choices have no effect on the quality of the work.
C) accepting, as it has helped preserve skills that might otherwise have disappeared.
D) enthusiastic, as it represents the ideal balance between tradition and modernity.
questions.explanation: The writer notes that both traditional and adapted approaches 'have allowed skills to survive that might otherwise have vanished' — a positive, accepting tone.

questions.question: When Delia says 'you shouldn't be able to tell where the old work ends and mine begins', she means that
options: A) she tries to make her repairs invisible so the piece looks unrestored.
B) her work is of equal quality to that of the original craftsperson.
C) she aims to blend her work seamlessly with the original as the goal of repair.
D) she is not interested in receiving personal recognition for her repairs.
questions.explanation: Delia explains this is 'the point of the craft' — the repair should blend seamlessly with the original, which is the defining aim of her work.
